skill.name: bash-pro
skill.description: Defensive Bash scripting guidance for production automation, CI/CD pipelines, and system utilities.
skill.summary: Create safer, more portable Bash scripts with testing, linting, logging, and CI guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, network, sudo, and weak-crypto patterns. Manual review found these are Markdown examples, tool names, and reference links in a defensive Bash guide, not executable skill code. The main residual risk is that users may ask the model to generate shell scripts that run powerful commands, so generated scripts require review before execution.

low_findings.title: External Command Patterns Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static external command findings are true pattern matches but false positives for malware. The file is Markdown guidance that recommends defensive shell practices, command validation, safe argument handling, and review of sudo usage rather than executing commands itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched content is contained in prose and inline Markdown examples. No script file, hook, or executable code path is present in the scanned skill.

low_findings.title: Reference URLs Are Not Network Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The network findings are hardcoded documentation links to public Bash, style guide, and tool resources. No evidence found that the skill sends data, credentials, prompts, or environment values to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in a References and Further Reading section. There are no fetch, curl, webhook, or upload instructions tied to user data.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Patterns Are Defensive Guidance
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings point to examples for safe temporary file creation, command detection, and cleanup traps. These are legitimate Bash hardening recommendations, but users should still inspect generated scripts before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are safety patterns using mktemp, command checks, and cleanup traps. They are not executed by the skill and do not target sensitive files.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Detections Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts map to prose lines and headings, not actual cryptographic API usage or hash commands. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, insecure random generation, or credential handling logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found defensive scripting prose at the cited locations. The file contains no implementation that performs cryptographic operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Shell Command Examples Require Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes safe-form examples for destructive operations and temporary directory cleanup. These examples are appropriate for Bash guidance, but generated scripts should be reviewed before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are examples in documentation, but their operational effect can be destructive if copied into scripts without correct variable control.

dangerous_patterns.title: Parallel Command Execution Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide references parallel execution with xargs. This is legitimate for automation, but the command supplied to parallel execution must be fixed or carefully validated.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is documentation-only, but xargs can amplify command injection or destructive behavior when used with untrusted input.

content.value_statement: Bash automation often fails through unsafe input handling, weak error control, and untested platform assumptions. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce defensive scripts with linting, tests, and CI checks.
actual_capabilities: Designs Bash scripts with strict mode, traps, safe argument parsing, and clear failure behavior.
Reviews shell scripts for quoting, globbing, input validation, cleanup, and portability problems.
Adds test strategy using Bats, ShellSpec, ShellCheck, shfmt, and CI workflow checks.
Explains Bash version choices, GNU versus BSD tool differences, and cross-platform safeguards.
Suggests structured logging, dry-run behavior, and safer handling of temporary resources.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not execute, sandbox, or verify generated scripts by itself.
Generated commands can still be dangerous if the user requests destructive automation.
It focuses on Bash and is not intended for PowerShell or complex application logic.
Security-sensitive scripts still need human review and testing in a controlled environment.

use_cases.title: Review CI Shell Steps
use_cases.description: Find quoting, exit handling, dependency, and portability problems before scripts run in a pipeline.

use_cases.title: Create Safe Automation Utilities
use_cases.description: Draft command-line scripts with usage output, validation, dry-run support, cleanup traps, and tests.

use_cases.title: Modernize Legacy Bash
use_cases.description: Improve old scripts with clearer structure, safer arrays, logging, static analysis, and Bash version checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Small Script
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Bash script for safety, quoting, error handling, and portability. Explain the most important fixes first.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Defensive Utility
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Bash utility for this task. Include argument parsing, dry-run mode, strict error handling, logging, cleanup, and usage text.

prompt_templates.title: Add Testing and Linting
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a practical test and lint plan for this Bash project. Include ShellCheck, formatting, Bats tests, and CI failure criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Production Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this production Bash workflow for unsafe external commands, filesystem risk, platform assumptions, observability gaps, and rollback needs.

output_examples.input: Review a deployment script before adding it to CI.
output: A prioritized safety review covering input validation, quoting, cleanup, dependency checks, and failure modes.
A short remediation plan with tests and lint checks for the deployment workflow.

output_examples.input: Create a file maintenance utility for a repository.
output: A structured script design with dry-run behavior, explicit options, safe path handling, and clear logs.
A companion test plan for normal paths, empty inputs, missing tools, and permission failures.

output_examples.input: Modernize a legacy shell automation folder.
output: A migration checklist that separates Bash-specific features from portable shell requirements.
Recommended lint, format, test, and CI steps for gradual adoption.

best_practices: Run generated scripts in dry-run mode or a disposable environment before using real data.
Treat all filenames, arguments, and environment values as untrusted input.
Pair generated Bash with ShellCheck, formatting, tests, and human review.
anti_patterns: Do not run generated destructive commands against production data without inspection.
Do not use this skill to hide unsafe command construction behind complex shell tricks.
Do not assume Linux-only commands will work on macOS or BSD systems.

faq.question: Does this skill run shell commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is a Markdown guide for generating and reviewing Bash scripts.

faq.question: Is it safe for production scripts?
faq.answer: It can improve production readiness, but generated scripts still need testing and review before use.

faq.question: Can it help with CI/CD pipelines?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers shell linting, formatting, tests, matrix checks, and pipeline safety practices.

faq.question: Does it support POSIX shell?
faq.answer: It can discuss portability, but the main focus is Bash rather than strict POSIX shell.

faq.question: What tools does it recommend?
faq.answer: It references ShellCheck, shfmt, Bats, ShellSpec, actionlint, Semgrep, and secrets scanners.

faq.question: Why did security scanning flag it?
faq.answer: The file contains many inline Bash examples and public reference links, which static scanning treats as potential command and network patterns.
